_note: Visual walk-throughs for the two rule areas new fans ask about most — offside and VAR — explained frame by frame instead of only in prose.

entries.title: Offside is judged when the pass is played
entries.summary: The freeze-frame moment is the passer's touch, not the runner's first touch later.

frames.label: Frame 1
frames.caption: The passer lifts their head and strikes the ball.
callouts: Ignore the later touch for the offside decision

frames.label: Frame 2
frames.caption: The runner arrives after the ball has already been played.
callouts: The timing question was already decided

entries.commonMistake: Fans often judge offside by where the attacker receives the pass instead of where they were when the pass began.

entries.title: It is the second-last defender, not always the goalkeeper
entries.summary: The keeper is usually one of the last two defenders, but not automatically.

frames.label: Frame 1
frames.caption: The goalkeeper has rushed out, leaving two field defenders deeper.
callouts: Use the second-last defender as the line

entries.commonMistake: People talk about being 'behind the keeper' when the real law is about the second-last opponent.

entries.title: VAR is a review tool, not a second referee running the whole match
entries.summary: VAR checks certain game-changing moments and either confirms the call or recommends an on-field review.

frames.label: Check
frames.caption: Video officials quietly review the incident while play is paused or continuing.

frames.label: Review
frames.caption: The referee may be sent to the monitor for a closer look.
callouts: Final decision still belongs to the referee

entries.commonMistake: Fans often think every foul can become a full VAR intervention when the review categories are narrower than that.
